content.text: What Qualifies as a Hotfix in Claude Applications

Not every production bug is a hotfix. A hotfix is an emergency patch that goes through an expedited but not skipped review process because waiting for the normal sprint cycle would cause meaningful harm to users or the business. The distinction matters because calling something a hotfix bypasses normal scheduling, demands immediate reviewer time, and creates pressure to cut corners — pressure that must be actively resisted.

A hotfix is not about moving fast and skipping gates. It is about moving the right gates to the front of the queue. The eval suite still runs; the prompt hash still gets registered. What changes is the timeline and the number of reviewers, not the standards.
Situation
Hotfix?
Reason
System outputs prohibited or harmful content
Yes — plus kill switch
Active harm; cannot wait for sprint cycle
SLO breached (error rate spike, latency > threshold)
Yes
Users experiencing degraded service right now
Must-not-do eval case failing in production
Yes
Behaviour spec violated in live system
Prompt producing off-brand but non-harmful responses
No
No active harm; normal fix/ branch with sprint prioritisation
Feature not working as intended (not harmful)
No
Normal priority; handled in next sprint
Cosmetic UI bug unrelated to AI output
No
No safety or SLO impact
Decision Tree and Flow Diagram

attrs.alt: Hotfix decision tree: prohibited output → kill switch; SLO breached or data at risk → expedited fix/; otherwise standard fix/. Hotfix steps: kill switch → fix/ branch → fix + eval case → full CI → merge to main → cherry-pick → smoke test → disable kill switch.

content.text: The decision tree has two gates. The first gate (is the system producing prohibited output?) controls whether to enable the kill switch — an immediate non-code action that takes effect within one request cycle. The second gate (is an SLO breached or is user data at risk?) determines whether the fix/ branch is expedited. Both paths that require a code fix still run the full eval suite before merge.
Step 1: Enable the Kill Switch

If the system is actively producing harmful output, the kill switch is the first action. It takes effect within one request cycle without requiring a code change or a new deployment. The on-call engineer can enable it from a phone.

The kill switch works because the application reads the flag value on every incoming request, not at startup. Changing the environment variable or config value causes the next request (and all subsequent ones) to follow the fallback code path immediately.

# Enable kill switch — effect within one request, no deploy needed

# Method A: AWS Parameter Store (if application uses SSM parameter polling)
aws ssm put-parameter   --name "/prod/FF_DISABLE_AI_TRIAGE"   --value "true"   --type "String"   --overwrite

# Method B: Kubernetes ConfigMap with hot-reload (watch-based reload in the app)
kubectl patch configmap claude-app-config   --type merge   --patch '{"data":{"FF_DISABLE_AI_TRIAGE":"true"}}'

# Method C: Feature flag service (LaunchDarkly, Flagsmith) via dashboard
# Toggle FF_DISABLE_AI to OFF in the web UI — takes effect within seconds

# Verify the flag is in effect (check health endpoint)
curl -s https://api.internal/health/flags |   python3 -c "import sys, json; d = json.load(sys.stdin); print('Kill switch:', d.get('FF_DISABLE_AI_TRIAGE', 'unknown'))"
# Expected: Kill switch: true

# Confirm the fallback is being returned (test one request)
curl -s -X POST https://api.internal/triage   -H "Content-Type: application/json"   -d '{"message": "test"}' |   python3 -c "import sys, json; d = json.load(sys.stdin); print(d.get('response', '')[:80])"
# Expected: "Our AI assistant is temporarily unavailable. Please contact support..."

Once the kill switch is on, users receive a graceful fallback response such as routing to a human agent or showing a maintenance message. The team now has time to diagnose the root cause properly without the system continuing to produce harmful output.
Step 2: Open the Hotfix Branch from Trunk

Open the hotfix branch from trunk HEAD, not from the release branch. If the fix also needs to go to a release branch, that happens via cherry-pick after the trunk fix is merged. Opening from trunk ensures the fix will be in all future releases automatically.

# Always start from fresh trunk HEAD
git fetch origin main
git checkout main
git pull --ff-only origin main

# If pull fails with a non-fast-forward error, do not force.
# Investigate why the local main has diverged before continuing.

# Branch name: fix/INCIDENT-<ID>-<short-description>
# The incident ID makes the branch name searchable in git log and PR history
git checkout -b fix/INCIDENT-2025-42-confidence-overflow

# Confirm you are on the right branch and at the right HEAD
git log --oneline -3
# Should show the latest trunk commits, not any release branch commits
Step 3: Write the Fix and Add an Eval Case

Before writing the code fix, write the eval case that reproduces the bug. This discipline has two purposes: it forces a precise understanding of the failure mode before touching any code, and it ensures the eval suite can never silently allow the same failure to return in a future prompt change.

The rule is: the new eval case must fail against the current (broken) code, and it must pass against the fixed code. If both are true, you have a reliable regression test. If the case passes against the broken code, you have not identified the correct eval for this failure.

# ============================================================
# scripts/add_incident_eval_case.py
# Run this BEFORE writing the code fix.
# ============================================================
import json
from pathlib import Path
from datetime import datetime, timezone

def add_incident_case(
    spec_path: str,
    case_id: str,
    description: str,
    input_text: str,
    must_not_do: list,
    must_do_signals: list,
    incident_id: str,
    tags: list = None,
) -> None:
    spec = json.loads(Path(spec_path).read_text())

    # Verify this case does not already exist
    existing_ids = {c["id"] for c in spec.get("eval_cases", [])}
    if case_id in existing_ids:
        print(f"Case {case_id} already exists — check for duplicates.")
        return

    new_case = {
        "id": case_id,
        "description": description,
        "input": input_text,
        "must_not_do": must_not_do,
        "must_do_signals": must_do_signals,
        "tags": tags or ["incident"],
        "added_by": incident_id,
        "added_at": datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
    }

    spec.setdefault("eval_cases", []).append(new_case)
    Path(spec_path).write_text(json.dumps(spec, indent=2))
    print(f"Added case {case_id} to {spec_path}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    add_incident_case(
        spec_path="specs/eval_suite.json",
        case_id="INCIDENT-2025-42-nil-confidence",
        description=(
            "When confidence score is None (tool call failure), system must "
            "still escalate rather than silently skipping the gate"
        ),
        input_text="My order has not arrived and it has been three weeks",
        must_not_do=["I cannot help with that", "I do not know"],
        must_do_signals=["specialist", "help you", "connect"],
        incident_id="INCIDENT-2025-42",
        tags=["escalation", "confidence-gate", "nil-safety"],
    )

# ============================================================
# Verify the case FAILS before the fix (confirms correct diagnosis)
# ============================================================
# python3 scripts/run_evals.py --full
# Expected output: FAIL INCIDENT-2025-42-nil-confidence
#   VIOLATION (must-not-do): I cannot help with that

# ============================================================
# src/escalation.py — the root cause: None bypasses the gate
# ============================================================

# BEFORE (broken): None < 0.6 raises TypeError in Python 3.x
# but in some environments it returns False, silently bypassing escalation
def should_escalate_broken(confidence: float) -> bool:
    return confidence < 0.6

# AFTER (fixed): explicit None check with safe default
def should_escalate(confidence: float | None) -> bool:
    """Return True if the conversation should be escalated to a human specialist.

    When confidence is None (e.g. tool call returned no score), we escalate
    by default because it is safer to over-escalate than to under-escalate.
    """
    if confidence is None:
        return True   # Safe default: unknown confidence → escalate
    return confidence < 0.6

# ============================================================
# Verify the case PASSES after the fix
# ============================================================
# python3 scripts/run_evals.py --full
# Expected output: PASS INCIDENT-2025-42-nil-confidence

# ============================================================
# Update prompt hash if the fix also changed the system prompt
# ============================================================
import hashlib, json
from pathlib import Path

def update_prompt_hash(incident_id: str, registry_path: str = "prompt_registry.json") -> None:
    prompt_text = Path("prompts/system_prompt.json").read_text()
    new_hash = hashlib.sha256(prompt_text.encode()).hexdigest()[:16]

    registry = json.loads(Path(registry_path).read_text())
    old_hash = registry.get("current_hash", "")

    if old_hash == new_hash:
        print("Prompt unchanged — hash update not needed for this hotfix.")
        return

    registry["previous_hash"] = old_hash
    registry["current_hash"] = new_hash
    registry["updated_for"] = incident_id
    registry["updated_at"] = __import__("datetime").datetime.utcnow().isoformat() + "Z"
    Path(registry_path).write_text(json.dumps(registry, indent=2))
    print(f"Prompt hash updated: {old_hash} → {new_hash}")
    print(f"Commit prompt_registry.json alongside the prompt change.")

update_prompt_hash("INCIDENT-2025-42")
Step 4: Expedited CI — Full Eval Suite Required

Expedited means a human reviewer is available immediately and the PR is merged the same day. It does not mean skipping CI gates. Every gate runs; only the timeline and reviewer availability are compressed.
Lint and type-check
: must pass. Syntax errors in a hotfix are embarrassing and avoidable.
Full eval suite including new INCIDENT case
: must pass. If the new case fails, the fix is not complete.
Drift detection
: prompt hash must match the registry. If the prompt changed, the registry must have been updated in step 3.
One reviewer approval
: the on-call engineer reviews within 30 minutes. Normal PRs require two reviewers; hotfixes require one, not zero.

If the eval suite fails on a hotfix branch, do not merge under any circumstances. Fix the failure and re-run. A hotfix that introduces a new must-not-do violation while fixing the original one is strictly worse than the original state.

# On the hotfix branch, before opening the PR:

# Run lint
ruff check . && mypy src/ --ignore-missing-imports

# Run the full eval suite locally first (faster feedback than waiting for CI)
python3 scripts/run_evals.py --full --fail-on-must-not-do

# Run drift check
python3 scripts/detect_drift.py

# Only open the PR when all three pass locally
# CI will re-run the same checks — this is not optional redundancy, it is
# the record that the gates were run on the exact code being merged.
gh pr create   --title "fix(INCIDENT-2025-42): guard nil confidence in escalation gate"   --body "Hotfix for INCIDENT-2025-42. Adds eval case that reproduces the nil confidence bypass. Fixes should_escalate() to treat None as escalate=True."   --label "emergency-hotfix"   --reviewer on-call-engineer-username
Step 5: Merge to Trunk and Cherry-Pick to Release Branches

After CI passes and the reviewer approves, merge to trunk via the PR. Then cherry-pick to any release branches that contain the affected code path. Not all hotfixes need cherry-picking — if the affected function does not exist in a release branch (it was added after the branch was cut), skip that branch.

# After the PR merges to main
git checkout main && git pull --ff-only origin main
FIX_SHA=$(git log main -1 --format="%H")

# Check which release branches contain the affected code path
for BRANCH in release/2025-Q2-v1 release/2025-Q3-v1; do
    if git show "origin/${BRANCH}:src/escalation.py" 2>/dev/null | grep -q "should_escalate"; then
        echo "${BRANCH}: cherry-pick needed"
    else
        echo "${BRANCH}: affected code not present — skip"
    fi
done

# Cherry-pick to each branch that needs it
git checkout release/2025-Q2-v1
git cherry-pick "$FIX_SHA"

# Full eval suite on the release branch (release prompt context may differ)
python3 scripts/run_evals.py --full --fail-on-must-not-do

git tag v1.2.3
git push origin release/2025-Q2-v1
git push origin v1.2.3
Step 6: Smoke Test and Disable Kill Switch

Wait for the fixed version to deploy before disabling the kill switch. Then run a smoke test against the live environment using the exact eval case that was added for this incident. Only disable the kill switch if the smoke test passes.

# scripts/smoke_test.py
import anthropic, json, sys
from pathlib import Path

def run_smoke_test(test_case_id: str, spec_path: str = "specs/eval_suite.json") -> bool:
    """Run a specific eval case against the live system prompt.

    This verifies the deployed code (not just the code in the repository)
    handles the specific failing input that triggered the incident.
    """
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    spec = json.loads(Path(spec_path).read_text())

    case = next((c for c in spec["eval_cases"] if c["id"] == test_case_id), None)
    if not case:
        print(f"Eval case '{test_case_id}' not found in spec.")
        return False

    prompt_data = json.loads(Path("prompts/system_prompt.json").read_text())

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=512,
        system=prompt_data["system"],
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": case["input"]}]
    )
    output = response.content[0].text

    violations = [rule for rule in case.get("must_not_do", []) if rule.lower() in output.lower()]
    missing_signals = [sig for sig in case.get("must_do_signals", []) if sig.lower() not in output.lower()]

    if violations:
        print(f"FAIL: must-not-do violations: {violations}")
        return False
    if missing_signals:
        print(f"FAIL: must-do signals missing: {missing_signals}")
        return False

    print(f"PASS: {test_case_id} — all checks clear")
    return True

if __name__ == "__main__":
    import argparse
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("--test-case", required=True, help="Eval case ID to run")
    args = parser.parse_args()
    ok = run_smoke_test(args.test_case)
    if ok:
        print("Safe to disable kill switch: python3 scripts/disable_kill_switch.py")
    else:
        print("DO NOT disable kill switch — smoke test failed.")
    sys.exit(0 if ok else 1)

# Run smoke test against the deployed system
python3 scripts/smoke_test.py --test-case INCIDENT-2025-42-nil-confidence

# If and only if it exits 0, disable the kill switch
if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
  aws ssm put-parameter     --name "/prod/FF_DISABLE_AI_TRIAGE"     --value "false"     --type "String"     --overwrite
  echo "Kill switch disabled at $(date -u). Monitor error rate for 30 minutes."
else
  echo "Smoke test failed — kill switch remains enabled. Diagnose further before disabling."
fi
Testing the Kill Switch Path in Unit Tests

The kill switch is only reliable if the application code correctly reads the flag on each request and returns the fallback response. Cover the kill switch path in unit tests so it cannot be broken silently by a future refactor.

import unittest, os
from unittest.mock import patch, MagicMock

class TestKillSwitchBehaviour(unittest.TestCase):

    def test_kill_switch_on_returns_fallback_and_does_not_call_claude(self):
        """When the kill switch is enabled, handle_request must not make any Claude API call."""
        from app import handle_request
        with patch.dict(os.environ, {"FF_DISABLE_AI_TRIAGE": "true"}):
            with patch("anthropic.Anthropic") as mock_client:
                result = handle_request(user_id="user-test", message="Help me with my order")

        # Claude must not be called
        mock_client.assert_not_called()

        # Response must be the graceful fallback, not an error
        self.assertIn("temporarily unavailable", result.lower())
        # Users must be given a path to human support
        self.assertTrue(
            "contact" in result.lower() or "support" in result.lower(),
            f"Fallback response must direct users to support, got: {result}"
        )

    def test_kill_switch_off_calls_claude_normally(self):
        """When the kill switch is disabled, handle_request must call the Claude API."""
        from app import handle_request
        with patch.dict(os.environ, {"FF_DISABLE_AI_TRIAGE": "false"}):
            with patch("anthropic.Anthropic") as mock_client:
                mock_block = MagicMock()
                mock_block.text = "I can help you with that order issue."
                mock_client.return_value.messages.create.return_value.content = [mock_block]
                result = handle_request(user_id="user-test", message="Help me with my order")

        mock_client.return_value.messages.create.assert_called_once()

    def test_kill_switch_is_evaluated_on_each_request_not_cached(self):
        """The flag value must be read from os.environ on each call, not cached at import time.

        If it were cached, enabling the kill switch would have no effect on in-flight requests.
        """
        from feature_flags import read_flags

        with patch.dict(os.environ, {"FF_DISABLE_AI_TRIAGE": "false"}):
            flags_off = read_flags("user-abc")

        with patch.dict(os.environ, {"FF_DISABLE_AI_TRIAGE": "true"}):
            flags_on = read_flags("user-abc")

        self.assertFalse(flags_off.disable_ai_triage)
        self.assertTrue(flags_on.disable_ai_triage)

    def test_kill_switch_fallback_is_not_an_error_response(self):
        """The fallback must be a human-readable message, not a 500 error or empty string."""
        from app import handle_request
        with patch.dict(os.environ, {"FF_DISABLE_AI_TRIAGE": "true"}):
            result = handle_request(user_id="user-test", message="Any message")

        self.assertGreater(len(result), 20, "Fallback response must be a meaningful message")
        self.assertNotIn("500", result)
        self.assertNotIn("Internal Server Error", result)
        self.assertNotIn("error", result.lower()[:30])
Monitoring: Alert When Kill Switch Has Been On Too Long

A kill switch that is enabled and not actively monitored can be forgotten. Users remain on the fallback path indefinitely while the engineering team focuses on the fix and forgets to re-enable the feature. Set an alert that fires if the kill switch has been on for more than 30 minutes without a fix being deployed.

# scripts/monitor_kill_switch.py
# Run every 5 minutes via cron or CI schedule

import sys
from datetime import datetime, timezone

def check_kill_switch_age(param_name: str = "/prod/FF_DISABLE_AI_TRIAGE",
                           alert_threshold_minutes: int = 30) -> None:
    import boto3
    ssm = boto3.client("ssm", region_name="us-east-1")

    response = ssm.get_parameter(Name=param_name, WithDecryption=False)
    param = response["Parameter"]
    value = param["Value"]
    last_modified = param["LastModifiedDate"]

    if value != "true":
        print(f"Kill switch is OFF. Last changed: {last_modified.isoformat()}")
        return

    age_minutes = (datetime.now(timezone.utc) - last_modified).total_seconds() / 60
    print(f"Kill switch is ON. Age: {age_minutes:.0f} minutes.")

    if age_minutes > alert_threshold_minutes:
        alert_message = (
            f"ALERT: FF_DISABLE_AI_TRIAGE has been enabled for "
            f"{age_minutes:.0f} minutes (threshold: {alert_threshold_minutes}m). "
            "If the hotfix is deployed, run: python3 scripts/smoke_test.py --test-case <INCIDENT-ID> "
            "and then disable the kill switch."
        )
        print(alert_message)
        # Replace with your alerting integration:
        # send_slack_alert(alert_message)
        # send_pagerduty_alert(alert_message)
        sys.exit(1)
    else:
        print(f"Within grace period ({age_minutes:.0f}/{alert_threshold_minutes} min). No alert.")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    check_kill_switch_age()
Hotfix Post-Mortem Requirements

Every hotfix that required a kill switch must produce a post-mortem within 48 hours of the incident being resolved. The post-mortem is not for blame assignment — it is for identifying what eval case, monitoring alert, or process step was missing that allowed the issue to reach production.
Section
What to write
Timeline
When issue first seen, kill switch on, fix deployed, smoke test passed, kill switch off
Root cause
Technical reason the bad output was produced (e.g. None confidence bypassed < 0.6 gate)
Eval gap
Which case was missing; why (input pattern not in eval set; boundary case not covered)
Fix applied
Code or prompt change; which files were changed
Prevention
New eval case(s) added; what monitoring alert would have caught this earlier

The most important deliverable of a post-mortem for a Claude application is the new eval case. If the failing input is not in the eval suite after the post-mortem, the same class of failure can recur silently on a future prompt or code change. A post-mortem that does not produce a new eval case is incomplete.
Common Pitfalls
Pitfall
Symptom
Fix
Skipping eval suite under pressure
Hotfix introduces a second must-not-do violation while fixing the first
Expedite the reviewer, not the tests — eval suite must run
Kill switch left on after fix deploys
Users stuck on fallback for hours or days after incident resolved
Smoke test plus explicit kill-switch-disable step in post-mortem checklist
Fix written on release branch not trunk
Fix present in Q2 release, missing from Q3 and trunk; regresses in next release
Always fix on trunk first; cherry-pick to release branches
No eval case added for the failing input
Same failure class returns silently on a future prompt change
Post-mortem is incomplete until the failing input is in the eval suite
Kill switch not tested in unit tests
Kill switch enabled in production but app still calls Claude (flag read bug)
Unit-test the kill switch path; verify flag is read at request time not cached
Cherry-pick assumed to be safe without re-running evals
Fix passes evals on trunk but breaks must-not-do on release branch (different prompt)
Run full eval suite independently on each release branch after cherry-pick
Summary

A hotfix in a Claude application is an emergency patch for situations where the system is producing prohibited output, an SLO is breached, or a must-not-do eval case is failing in production. The first action for active harmful output is enabling the kill switch — no code change, takes effect within one request cycle, can be done from a phone. The hotfix branch opens from trunk HEAD, includes a new eval case that reproduces the bug (verified to fail before the fix and pass after it), runs the full eval suite before merge (no shortcuts), and then cherry-picks to affected release branches with independent eval runs on each. The kill switch is disabled only after a smoke test against the live environment confirms the specific failing case passes. A 30-minute kill-switch alert prevents forgotten flags. Every kill-switch incident produces a post-mortem within 48 hours, with the new eval case as the primary deliverable. The kill switch path is covered by unit tests so it can be trusted when needed most.
